title: Multiple Definition Again
body:
The bug I meet is similar to #4416, however, the difference point is that objects with different types are assigned with same name.

```
/usr/bin/ld: VTestbench__ALL.a(VTestbench___024root__DepSet_h17da5287__0__Slow.o): in function `VTestbench___024root___eval_initial__TOP__0(VTestbench___024root*)':
VTestbench___024root__DepSet_h17da5287__0__Slow.cpp:(.text+0x437): multiple definition of `VTestbench___024root___eval_initial__TOP__0(VTestbench___024root*)';
VTestbench__ALL.a(VTestbench___024root__DepSet_h17da5287__0.o):VTestbench___024root__DepSet_h17da5287__0.cpp:(.text+0x9a45): first defined here
```

In `VTestbench___024root__DepSet_h17da5287__0__Slow.cpp`, there is a `VL_ATTR_COLD void VTestbench___024root___eval_initial__TOP__0(VTestbench___024root* vlSelf)`, while the type of the `VTestbench___024root___eval_initial__TOP__0` in `VTestbench___024root__DepSet_h17da5287__0.cpp` is `VL_INLINE_OPT VlCoroutine VTestbench___024root___eval_initial__TOP__0(VTestbench___024root* vlSelf)`.

I use the latest v5.018 verilator.

Sorry, I cannot provide a testcase as this is from a private project. However, I can provide a stripped version if necessary. The bug occurs while using macros to conditionally define signals.
